differences.title: 1. TEAM ORGANIZATION
differences.leftDesc: Teams own specific features or domains, enabling autonomy and faster development cycles.
differences.rightDesc: One team manages the entire frontend, potentially leading to coordination overhead.

differences.title: 2. TECHNOLOGY STACK
differences.leftDesc: Each micro frontend can use its own tech stack, optimizing for specific needs.
differences.rightDesc: The entire application shares a single technology stack, simplifying maintenance.

differences.title: 3. DEPLOYMENT
differences.leftDesc: Features can be deployed independently, reducing risk and enabling faster releases.
differences.rightDesc: All changes are deployed together, requiring extensive coordination and testing.

differences.title: 4. SCALABILITY
differences.leftDesc: Scale individual parts of the application based on their specific load.
differences.rightDesc: Scale the entire application, potentially over-provisioning resources.

differences.title: 5. CODEBASE SIZE
differences.leftDesc: Each micro frontend is a smaller, more manageable codebase.
differences.rightDesc: The entire application resides in one large, potentially complex, codebase.

differences.title: 6. COMPLEXITY
differences.leftDesc: Managing the communication and orchestration between multiple micro frontends adds complexity.
differences.rightDesc: Complexity is contained within a single large codebase.

differences.title: 7. MAINTENANCE
differences.leftDesc: Easier to refactor or replace small, independent parts.
differences.rightDesc: Maintaining a large, interconnected codebase can become difficult over time.
